speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So the last thing you said to us at the end of the reel was the yellow star with Jude that you had--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jude, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --sewn as a patch.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was immediately?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- first I think there was the arm band, right, and then they changed it. I don't know exactly when, but I think it came quickly after. So-- and then also-- the Poles took advantage of it, too, because they could see-- many of them sided with the Germans. And they were-- as I said before, there was-- we knew that there was always some kind of anti-Semitism existing in Poland, but we-- but it was more in the open then, in the place where we lived. And so-- the Poles also-- there was a curfew. And, of course, the Poles would turn Jews in [INAUDIBLE] run over to a city of [INAUDIBLE] or somebody, in the evening, at night time. And we couldn't-- we were limited to-- not limited-- we were forbidden to be on the street or go any place beyond the curfew time. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And it was really the turning point of the city of Oswiecim, when it became Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The Polish army, I understand, had a big camp just outside of Oswiecim, which was part of-- must've been part of Auschwitz-- Oswiecim. And they-- so the Germans when they turned-- they had a-- they had all the buildings that he made to turn this place into a concentration camp. I don't know if it was a division or it was a like, here in our country, in the United States, they are like-- I was stationed in the Fourth Army, when I was in the Army during the Korean War. But maybe there they had-- I don't know what-- I was too young to really know what the setup was, but it was a large cavalry type of Army camp, which the-- the Germans turned into the-- to the place for the-- The stables became our homes when-- when I was in Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The-- so-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] this existed-- this-- we were in Auschwitz until 1949-- '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you tell me-- in the city itself, you remained in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the town, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family in the apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But was there a ghetto? Did you-- were you forced into smaller living quarters? What did you do for food during this-- from 1939 till 1941, what was the-- what were your living conditions like? Where were you living?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in the same place. I-- I don't remember about food, there was-- somehow we had enough food, I think. And we did not take any-- any-- we didn't have anybody else living with us. So the living conditions only changed that we were restricted to because of the curfew and also because you could not do certain things because you were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like Jude, you could not-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think they confiscated all the Jewish businesses. And turned them over to the Poles. The-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember that we stayed in the same apartment until we were resettled at that time in Sosnowiec in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before you got to Sosnowiec, what about your radio? What about your gramophone? Did you have a bicycle? Were things taken? What things were taken from you or did you have to turn in because of the laws? Do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really remember. If-- maybe because we didn't really-- we did not have any like automobiles. I'm sure you had to turn in, and they confiscated businesses, automobiles and-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: But your radio and the gramophone, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I-- I really don't know. I don't-- I think that we-- I don't remember having the radio after that. Maybe we were not allowed to listen to it, or maybe they took it. Or-- but I remember that we kept the gramophone, because-- this during that time, we-- my parents wanted all the friends of-- of my siblings, especially the older ones, to come to our place rather than they, because of the curfew, because they were concerned. The Germans would also [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] beat up on you for no reason at all, hit you with a rifle, maybe kick you, or-- and say you, [INAUDIBLE] Jude. And so that's why the concern was for them so to-- keep the family at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you, yourself-- did you-- did you-- were you beaten up or anything by any German soldiers at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, no. But I know that my brother did. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Romek. Romek was beaten up, but he also-- he also used to go out and look for them by themselves. They used to really beat the hell out of them by singly. And then he used to run, come home, and because he was very much, very angered about the whole situation. And he was like a daredevil.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he used to always run at home, and then he says, Mother, hide me someplace, because they're coming to look for me. Because-- one time I remember he ran home because they-- they throw a cigarette butt, burning cigarette butt, in-- in behind this shirt. So he-- He threw the cigarette butt or someone threw it at him?

speaker: interviewee
text: A German soldier threw, you know, right inside his-- the back of his-- inside his shirt, and he was hurting very much. So he came running home one time, and screaming. But he-- he would-- he-- he would go out and during curfew, and the parents, he would say, he was going to see a cousin in the same building, but actually he-- he must have snuck out. And-- because he was looking for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think he was part of the resistance group or partisan--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I don't think at that time there was a resistance group in my hometown. I heard that resistance groups may-- when-- when we moved to Sosnowiec. But in our hometown, there was mainly Jewish organizations, like the girls belonged to the Bais Yaakov [INAUDIBLE]. And my brothers, Ramek and Herman maybe to one of the men's organizations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like Betar or--

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Gredonia ?] yeah, or-- something-- but I-- they were-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they-- they were organizations that were mainly [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] connected somehow with Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Zionist organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zionist-- Zionist organizations. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: I also wanted to ask you, during this period, you said you were-- you have no-- I guess you don't really remember about the food, but what about, even before the war, were there movie theaters in your town? Were you able to go to the movies, and did they show anti-Semitic films in Oswiecim?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember going to a movie, ever. But I'm sure there must have been some movies in town.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was wondering, you know, so they showed-- if they showed any German films then, you know, how they should-- they're-- they've become infamous now, those German films.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, especially which was the-- I remember reading about it more, and also pictures in the paper than the movies because we didn't-- there was no-- you didn't have a television. Or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any kind of Yiddish theater or Yiddish music? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, there was-- there was Yiddish music, Yiddish theater, which I-- I have not seen it. Because-- but I know that there was comedies like [INAUDIBLE]. And-- [INAUDIBLE]-- I can't really think of too many of those.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get-- did that continue? Or-- was that disbanded?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was disbanded because they-- you could not congregate. The Jews were not allowed to-- to congregate in a group.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by 1941, when you-- when your family was sent to Sosnowiec, what was going-- were you aware of anything that was going on in terms of Jews being pushed some--

speaker: interviewee
text: No question. I was aware of it before. I was aware that what was happening in Germany, that the Jews were being-- being persecuted, and when they burned the books and they-- and where they demolished all the Jewish businesses and stores and some-- and the synagogues were destroyed. I was very well aware of that. And I-- I saw our temple burning, when-- when the-- when the Germans burned it in 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel then? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt like, how could this happen? Why-- why-- why this-- God letting this happen? Our-- our place of worship, this is-- you know, the main-- the main synagogue of town, which has been-- I don't know exactly how old it was. But it was a nice and beautiful synagogue. It was-- a-- there were other synagogues-- there were other [INAUDIBLE], what they called, and also house of prayers, mainly [INAUDIBLE], where more Hasidic than ultra Orthodox people would-- would go. Synagogue was already more sort of, I would, say reform. But it was maybe a little bit more modern because it was a beautiful structure. It had [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE]. A [INAUDIBLE] usually had benches and tables and maybe a little credenza to hold the Sefer Torah. But-- or some kind of a arc, but the synagogue had to be beautiful [INAUDIBLE] with the arc with the-- in the Sefer Torah. So-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: I was asking about-- you were telling me about the burning of the synagogue, and I was asking you how you felt about it. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt like-- like-- like the-- this is a place where I was going to pray, where I was going to school and learn about the [INAUDIBLE], Russia and-- and about the five books of Moses, [INAUDIBLE]. And that this-- what am I going to do now? Where-- where-- where do I go from here? I felt like I was-- there was a terrible violation to-- to my education.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you going to public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was going to public school, too. I-- I was going to elementary school, which was total opposite direction from the-- from the synagogue, but since we were so centrally located, so for me it was maybe a little bit further to the public school, but we-- then-- then went to the synagogue. Synagogue was very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: So while the-- so from 1939 to 1941, you continued to go to public school. You weren't banned?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, the schools-- no, you were not allowed to go to school. I don't even think that the schools were open. Yeah. And after the Germans occupied our town, I don't think so. I-- I think the learning ceased to exist.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you did say earlier that you were bar mitzvah'd, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was bar mitzvah'd in the basement of my grandfather's house, and-- and many people came. But it was not-- never the same as what would have really happened in our main-- in our temple, main sanctuary. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] sorry--

speaker: interviewer
text: I was asking about the bar mitzvah. What was that like for you? I mean, you described it a little earlier for us, if you remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was-- I studied very hard because I want my parents to be proud of me. And I also wanted to do a good job, because the-- of-- of what has been happening, to continue with my-- to carry on my-- my Jewish tradition. And so I worked harder and I-- I struggled, and I-- I don't personally remember. But I was told that I did a very good job. And my parents were very proud of me for doing what I did. But it was with candle lights, or-- and-- and those-- those-- the lights that you have with gasoline.

speaker: interviewer
text: An oil lamp? Or a gas lamp?

speaker: interviewee
text: A gas lamp with the cylinder, glass cylinder. And-- and-- not actually gas, but it was gasoline type, you know, like, they call it naphtha, I remember, in Polish. And that's how I remember having my bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your bar mitzvah done in secret because of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Top secret, in top secret. We had guards standing outside that were not quite visible to make sure that the Germans, because we would have to disperse because they would have probably arrested a lot of people, and-- and they would have beaten a lot of people as well. I don't know if they would have killed anybody at that time, but they would definitely do a lot of harm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how were you notified about going to Sosnowiec? And how did you get there? How did you know? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't remember exactly how we got there. I think we-- we must have gone by train. We were, at that time, they did not call-- I think-- that at that time, it was not-- I know that the city became Judenrein at that time, when we left Auschwitz or Oswiecim. But I think they called it more like a resettlement to the city of Sosnowiec. And we-- and life totally changed. We were cramped in a one, two room place. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like a-- we were just shoved in in two rooms, which were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] limited-- with limited bathrooms and wash-- and a place where you can wash up. It was-- life changed completely. And a few weeks later, a few months later, they started to take my older brother, my older sisters to-- to forced labor camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So every time [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a-- you had to report-- you know, they-- you would be taken to a labor camp. [INAUDIBLE] lager, they called it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I know that my parents were-- pulled a lot of strings somehow so that the girls would go to a women's camp, which they did. They go-- they went to the [INAUDIBLE], and two different camps, by the way, for the-- for my sisters. And [INAUDIBLE], which was part of Czechoslovakia, that-- but the Germans already occupied it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and they worked in a-- they worked in a-- in a factory sewing uniforms. And they were also guarded by women guards at that time. At the start, I understand, and we were able to write and get letters and-- and my-- I remember, when my parents would write to my sisters, tell them to be strong and to work hard and to be clean and neat, and-- because the [INAUDIBLE], you know, which is to the aunt, the-- the-- an aunt-- [INAUDIBLE] means freedom is-- is close. You know. And then life totally changed, you know. It was not the same. It was a time of sadness, because you-- they were taking the kids away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was left in the ghetto with you and your parents? Who was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they took--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Left? I was left with my-- and my younger brother, [INAUDIBLE] and I-- Joshua-- and I and my mother and father were left.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your grandparents and all your aunts and uncles? Where were all of them? Where did all of them go? Were they resettled in Sosnowiec?

words.text: Where did all of them go?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were resettled in Sosnowiec, too. Right. But-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it-- I don't think they resettled in the same building even.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for food? And-- and do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food was rationed. And-- and food was a big problem. And you could not go into certain places, because they said Juden forboden. So that means you cannot enter. So in the beginning, I would take my star off, and-- and we spoke German. We spoke Polish. And I would be able to buy a loaf of bread. But it-- food became a problem at the time. Also, the curfew was even much stricter than in Oswiecim. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the Poles were also very rough on the Jewish people. Yeah. And they-- because they had their stores were open. And they would-- they would-- they would point out if you-- if they knew that you-- and-- and then you were sent to prison for doing-- for violating the-- to go into place where it said Juden forboden. And the-- it was a big building that we were in Sosnowiec. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The-- I remember when we came to Sosnowiec, we were-- we came to a high school that was the place where they assigned different locations for you, where you [? assigned, ?] what I would call, some rooms that you can live in. And we were there for a few days in this-- it was very, very hot. I remember, I got very sick, because I was-- like I had like a sunstroke from that-- from being exposed to the sun and not having enough food. And-- and everything was a problem. Even water-- to get some water. So from this place, we were reassigned to locations in the Jewish part of Sosnowiec. Sosnowiec, there's a very large Jewish population. It was a much bigger city, than Oswiecim. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] And from Sosnowiec, then they took us to Srodula, to the ghetto, which was right outside the city of Sosnowiec.

words.text: able to buy a loaf of

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you walk there? or--

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- I think we might-- we walked there. We walked-- you have to leave everything, whatever you could carry, come clothes you were able to take.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you family was rather well-to-do, did your-- did your parents do anything in particular, like sew money into things, or jewels? Did your parents take from when they originally left Oswiecim? Was there anything they did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, whatever they could, they took. And it was-- it-- and that helped to get to buy, you know, things on the black market. Bread and-- and-- whatever food we could-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we could get at that time. But it was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it-- it was a rough time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you involved in the black market at all? Did you, because you were so young, you could get in and out, like you said how you would take off your star, and go get--

words.text: you involved in the black market at

speaker: interviewee
text: The star-- right. But that-- yeah, no, they-- no, I would not because my parents were very concerned about the children, and they kept us pretty much in-- sheltered

speaker: interviewer
text: So you left Sosnowiec, and you were taken to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Srodula.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto of Srodula. Srodula, we were resettled in-- It was even a worse condition because it was like a small village outside of Sosnowiec. And-- and they took most everything away from you, whatever you had, except they'd leave you with a suitcase. We were limited, too, because you also carrying this stuff. You were not-- so I remember that it was a cramped quarters. And it was very-- everything was limited. You couldn't take a bath. You couldn't-- you had to wait in line if you want to go to the bathroom, because there was a lot of people bunched together. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were working in the ghetto. My-- my father used to go to work every morning, and I was going to-- I was working in the factory, I think. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: For uniforms. I think I was sewing buttons on the uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: German uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: German uniforms. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then it didn't take that long, must have been already in-- it was in '43, when they started to liquidate the-- the ghetto, and they would transport Monday. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I came home, and it was the [INAUDIBLE]. It was a horrible time because while we were away, [SIGHS] they took my mother into Auschwitz. [CRYING] And this-- I never saw her-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I never saw her again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were left, my-- my father and my younger brother, Joshua. I don't know how they did not take him. Maybe he was hiding somewhere or playing with someone. But-- but they were starting to liquidate, so where we lived, there was like a place where they had an apartment complex, where there was a basement, so-- We're gonna have to stop here and change the reel. OK. I'm sorry.
